([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Santa Bárbara*, meaning '[Saint Barbara](/wiki/Saint_Barbara "Saint Barbara")') is a coastal city in [Santa Barbara County, California](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_County,_California "Santa Barbara County, California"), of which it is also the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"). Situated on a south-facing section of coastline, the longest such section on the [West Coast of the United States](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") excepting Alaska, the city lies between the steeply rising [Santa Ynez Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Ynez_Mountains "Santa Ynez Mountains") and the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). Santa Barbara's climate is often described as [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"), and the city has been dubbed "The American Riviera".[[11]](#cite_note-travel.nytimes.com-11) As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Santa Barbara had a population of 88,665.[[12]](#cite_note-Census2020PLLede-12)

**Santa Barbara** ([Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Santa Bárbara*, meaning '[Saint Barbara](/wiki/Saint_Barbara "Saint Barbara")') is a coastal city in [Santa Barbara County, California](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_County,_California "Santa Barbara County, California"), of which it is also the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat"). Situated on a south-facing section of coastline, the longest such section on the [West Coast of the United States](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States") excepting Alaska, the city lies between the steeply rising [Santa Ynez Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Ynez_Mountains "Santa Ynez Mountains") and the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"). Santa Barbara's climate is often described as [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"), and the city has been dubbed "The American Riviera".[[11]](#cite_note-travel.nytimes.com-11) As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Santa Barbara had a population of 88,665.[[12]](#cite_note-Census2020PLLede-12)

In addition to being a popular tourist and resort destination, the city has a diverse economy that includes a large service sector, education, technology, health care, finance, agriculture, manufacturing, and local government. In 2004, the service sector accounted for 35% of local employment.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2024, the biggest employer in Santa Barbara County was government, followed by private education and healthcare, then leisure and hospitality. Together, these accounted for 50.5% of all employment.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Area institutions of higher learning include the [University of California, Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara"), [Santa Barbara City College](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_City_College "Santa Barbara City College"), [Westmont College](/wiki/Westmont_College "Westmont College"), [The Colleges of Law](/wiki/The_Colleges_of_Law "The Colleges of Law"), and [Antioch University Santa Barbara](/wiki/Antioch_University_Santa_Barbara "Antioch University Santa Barbara").

The city is served by [Santa Barbara Municipal Airport](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Municipal_Airport "Santa Barbara Municipal Airport") and train service is provided by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), which operates two service lines with stops in Santa Barbara: the *[Pacific Surfliner](/wiki/Pacific_Surfliner "Pacific Surfliner")*, which runs from [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego") to [San Luis Obispo](/wiki/San_Luis_Obispo,_California "San Luis Obispo, California"), and the *[Coast Starlight](/wiki/Coast_Starlight "Coast Starlight")*, which runs from [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") to [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"). The Santa Barbara area is connected via [U.S. Highway 101](/wiki/U.S._Route_101_in_California "U.S. Route 101 in California") to [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") 100 mi (160 km) to the southeast and [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") 325 mi (525 km) to the northwest.

Behind the city, in and beyond the Santa Ynez Mountains, is the [Los Padres National Forest](/wiki/Los_Padres_National_Forest "Los Padres National Forest"), which contains several remote wilderness areas. [Channel Islands National Park](/wiki/Channel_Islands_National_Park "Channel Islands National Park") and [Channel Islands National Marine Sanctuary](/wiki/Channel_Islands_National_Marine_Sanctuary "Channel Islands National Marine Sanctuary") are located approximately 20 miles (30 km) offshore.

Evidence of human habitation of the area begins at least 13,000 years ago. Evidence for a [Paleoindian](/wiki/Paleo-Indians "Paleo-Indians") presence includes a fluted [Clovis](/wiki/Clovis_culture "Clovis culture")-like point found in the 1980s along the western Santa Barbara County coast, as well as the remains of [Arlington Springs Man](/wiki/Arlington_Springs_Man "Arlington Springs Man"), found on Santa Rosa Island in the 1960s. At least 25,000 [Chumash](/wiki/Chumash_people "Chumash people") natives lived in the region prior to Spanish contact.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Five Chumash villages flourished in the area. The present-day area of Santa Barbara City College was the village of *Mispu*; the site of the Los Baños pool (along west beach) was the village of *Syukhtun*, chief Yanonalit's large village located between Bath and Chapala streets; *Amolomol* was at the mouth of Mission Creek; and *Swetete*, above the bird refuge.[[16]](#cite_note-radic-16)

### Spanish era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Spanish era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Santa_Barbara_mission_CA1.jpg/250px-Santa_Barbara_mission_CA1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Barbara_mission_CA1.jpg)

[Mission Santa Barbara](/wiki/Mission_Santa_Barbara "Mission Santa Barbara"), known as "the Queen of the Missions", was founded by the Spanish in 1786.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/SB_Presidio.jpg/250px-SB_Presidio.jpg)](/wiki/File:SB_Presidio.jpg)

[Presidio of Santa Barbara](/wiki/Presidio_of_Santa_Barbara "Presidio of Santa Barbara")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Jos%C3%A9_de_la_Guerra_y_Noriega_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Jos%C3%A9_de_la_Guerra_y_Noriega_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jos%C3%A9_de_la_Guerra_y_Noriega_(cropped).jpg)

Don [José de la Guerra y Noriega](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_de_la_Guerra_y_Noriega "José de la Guerra y Noriega") served as Commandant of the [Presidio of Santa Barbara](/wiki/Presidio_of_Santa_Barbara "Presidio of Santa Barbara") and founded the [Guerra family of California](/wiki/Guerra_family_of_California "Guerra family of California"), a prominent [Californio](/wiki/Californio "Californio") family which produced numerous Mayors of Santa Barbara, California Senators, and more.

The explorer [Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo](/wiki/Juan_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Cabrillo "Juan Rodríguez Cabrillo"), sailing for the [Kingdom of Spain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Spain "Kingdom of Spain"), sailed through what is now called the [Santa Barbara Channel](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Channel "Santa Barbara Channel") in 1542, anchoring briefly in the area. In 1602, Spanish maritime explorer [Sebastián Vizcaíno](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_Vizca%C3%ADno "Sebastián Vizcaíno") gave the name "Santa Barbara" to the channel and also to one of the [Channel Islands](/wiki/Channel_Islands_of_California "Channel Islands of California").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

A land [expedition](/wiki/Portola_expedition "Portola expedition") led by [Gaspar de Portolà](/wiki/Gaspar_de_Portol%C3%A0 "Gaspar de Portolà") visited around 1769, and Franciscan missionary [Juan Crespi](/wiki/Juan_Crespi "Juan Crespi"), who accompanied the expedition, named a large native town "Laguna de la Concepción". Cabrillo's earlier name, however, is the one that has survived.

The first permanent European residents were Spanish missionaries and soldiers under [Felipe de Neve](/wiki/Felipe_de_Neve "Felipe de Neve"), who arrived in 1782 and constructed the [Presidio](/wiki/Presidio_of_Santa_Barbara "Presidio of Santa Barbara"). They were sent to both secure the Spanish claim to the region and to convert the [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_California "Indigenous peoples of California") to [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). Many of the Spaniards brought their families with them, and those formed the nucleus of the small town – at first just a cluster of adobes – that surrounded the [Presidio of Santa Barbara](/wiki/Presidio_of_Santa_Barbara "Presidio of Santa Barbara"). The Santa Barbara Mission was established on the Feast of [Saint Barbara](/wiki/Saint_Barbara "Saint Barbara"), December 4, 1786. It was the tenth of the California Missions to be founded by the Spanish Franciscans.[[18]](#cite_note-18) It was dedicated by Padre [Fermín Lasuén](/wiki/Ferm%C3%ADn_Lasu%C3%A9n "Fermín Lasuén"), who succeeded Padre [Junipero Serra](/wiki/Junipero_Serra "Junipero Serra") as the second president and founder of the California Franciscan Mission Chain. The Chumash laborers built a connection between the canyon creek and the Santa Barbara Mission water system through the use of a dam and an aqueduct.[[19]](#cite_note-19) During the following decades, many of the natives died of diseases such as [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), against which they had no [natural immunity](/wiki/Innate_immune_system "Innate immune system").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The most dramatic event of the Spanish period was the powerful [1812 earthquake](/wiki/1812_San_Juan_Capistrano_earthquake "1812 San Juan Capistrano earthquake"), and [tsunami](/wiki/Tsunami "Tsunami"), with an estimated magnitude of 7.1, which destroyed the Mission as well as the rest of the town; water reached as high as present-day Anapamu Street, and carried a ship half a mile up Refugio Canyon.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) The Mission was rebuilt by 1820 after the earthquake.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Following the earthquake, the Mission fathers chose to rebuild in a grander manner, and it is this construction that survives to the present day, the best-preserved of the California Missions, and still functioning as an active church by the Franciscans. After the Mexican government secularized the missions in the 1830s, the baptismal, marriage, and burial records of other missions were transferred to Santa Barbara, and are now found in the [Santa Barbara Mission Archive-Library](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Mission-Archive_Library "Santa Barbara Mission-Archive Library").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The Spanish period ended in 1822 with the conclusion of the [Mexican War of Independence](/wiki/Mexican_War_of_Independence "Mexican War of Independence"), which terminated 300 years of Spanish colonial rule and transferred control to the newly independent Mexican government.

Santa Barbara street names reflect the influence of the Spanish period. The names *de le Guerra* and *Carrillo* come from the [Guerra family of California](/wiki/Guerra_family_of_California "Guerra family of California") and [Carrillo family of California](/wiki/Carrillo_family_of_California "Carrillo family of California"), respectively. They were instrumental in building up the town, so they were honored by having streets named after them.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Mexican era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mexican era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse_Mural_room.jpg/250px-Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse_Mural_room.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse_Mural_room.jpg)

Mural Room (formerly Board of Supervisors' Hearing Room) within the Santa Barbara County Courthouse. Wall murals depict the history of Santa Barbara. The room is used occasionally as a courtroom.

After the forced [secularization of the Missions in 1833](/wiki/Mexican_secularization_act_of_1833 "Mexican secularization act of 1833"), successive [Mexican Governors](/wiki/Governor_of_Alta_California#Mexican_Governors_of_Alta_California "Governor of Alta California") distributed the large land tracts formerly held by the Franciscan Order to various families in order to reward service or build alliances. These land grants to local notable families mark the beginning of the "Rancho Period" in California and Santa Barbara history. Fernando Tico was one of the first settlers who received land grants for the local area. Fernando led the Native Americans against the Argentinian pirates in the 1800s. The population remained sparse, with enormous cattle operations run by wealthy families. It was during this period that [Richard Henry Dana Jr.](/wiki/Richard_Henry_Dana_Jr. "Richard Henry Dana Jr.") first visited Santa Barbara and wrote about the [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture") and people of Santa Barbara in his book *[Two Years Before the Mast](/wiki/Two_Years_Before_the_Mast "Two Years Before the Mast")*.

Santa Barbara fell bloodlessly to a battalion of [American soldiers](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") under [John C. Frémont](/wiki/John_C._Fr%C3%A9mont "John C. Frémont") on December 27, 1846, during the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), and after the [Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo](/wiki/Treaty_of_Guadalupe_Hidalgo "Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo") in 1848 it became part of the expanding United States.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Post-Conquest era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Post-Conquest era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Sant_Barbara_-_State_Street_1880s.jpg/250px-Sant_Barbara_-_State_Street_1880s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sant_Barbara_-_State_Street_1880s.jpg)

State Street Santa Barbara, California in the 1880s. A view looking north from Canon Perdido Street.

Change came quickly in Santa Barbara following the American [Conquest of California](/wiki/Conquest_of_California "Conquest of California"). The population doubled between 1850 and 1860. In 1851, land surveyor Salisbury Haley designed the street grid, famously botching the block measurements, misaligning the streets, thereby creating doglegs at certain intersections.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Wood construction replaced adobe as American settlers moved in; during the [Gold Rush years](/wiki/California_Gold_Rush "California Gold Rush") and following, the town became a haven for bandits and gamblers, and a dangerous and lawless place. Charismatic gambler and [highwayman](/wiki/Highwayman "Highwayman") [Jack Powers](/wiki/Jack_Powers "Jack Powers") had virtual control of the town in the early 1850s, until driven out by a posse organized in San Luis Obispo. English gradually supplanted Spanish as the language of daily life, becoming the language of official record in 1870.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The first newspaper, the *Santa Barbara Gazette*, was founded in 1855.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

While the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") had little effect on Santa Barbara, the disastrous drought of 1863 ended the Rancho Period, as most of the cattle died and ranchos were broken up and sold. Mortimer Cook, a wealthy entrepreneur, arrived in 1871 and opened the city's first bank. Cook later served two terms as mayor.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Cook founded the first National Gold Bank of Santa Barbara in 1873. The building of [Stearns Wharf](/wiki/Stearns_Wharf "Stearns Wharf") in 1872 enhanced Santa Barbara's commercial and tourist accessibility; previously goods and visitors had to transfer from [steamboats](/wiki/Steamboats "Steamboats") to smaller craft to row ashore. During the 1870s, writer [Charles Nordhoff](/wiki/Charles_Nordhoff_(journalist) "Charles Nordhoff (journalist)") promoted the town as a health resort and destination for well-to-do travelers from other parts of the U.S.; many of them came, and many stayed. The luxurious [Arlington Hotel](/wiki/Arlington_Hotel_(Santa_Barbara,_California) "Arlington Hotel (Santa Barbara, California)") dated from this period. In 1887 the railroad finally went through to Los Angeles, and in 1901 to San Francisco: Santa Barbara was now easily accessible by land and by sea, and subsequent development was brisk.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Santa Barbara had a [system of street railways](/wiki/Streetcars_in_Santa_Barbara,_California "Streetcars in Santa Barbara, California") that operated from 1875 through 1929. Begun as a single mule-drawn line from the waterfront pier to the Arlington Hotel, over the decades it was incrementally expanded, later electrified, and operated until its closure in June 1929.

[Peter J. Barber](/wiki/Peter_J._Barber "Peter J. Barber"), an architect, designed many Late Victorian style residences and served twice as mayor, in 1880 and again in 1890. A year after Barber's term as mayor, President Benjamin Harrison became the first of five presidents to visit Santa Barbara.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Early 20th century to World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Early 20th century to World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/SantaBarbaraVenturaOilGasFields.png/250px-SantaBarbaraVenturaOilGasFields.png)](/wiki/File:SantaBarbaraVenturaOilGasFields.png)

A map of Santa Barbara Oil and Gas Fields

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse%2C_California.jpg/250px-Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse%2C_California.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse,_California.jpg)

The new Santa Barbara County Courthouse was dedicated on August 14, 1929.

Just before the turn of the 20th century, oil was discovered at the [Summerland Oil Field](/wiki/Summerland_Oil_Field "Summerland Oil Field"), and the region along the beach east of Santa Barbara sprouted numerous oil derricks and piers for [drilling offshore](/wiki/Offshore_drilling "Offshore drilling"). This was the first offshore oil development in the world; oil drilling offshore would become a contentious practice in the Santa Barbara area, which continues to the present day.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Santa Barbara housed the world's largest movie studio during the era of silent film. Flying A Studios, a division of the [American Film Manufacturing Company](/wiki/American_Film_Manufacturing_Company "American Film Manufacturing Company"), operated on two city blocks centered at State and Mission between 1910 and 1922, with the industry shutting down locally and moving to [Hollywood](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles") once it outgrew the area, needing the resources of a larger city. Flying A and the other smaller local studios produced approximately 1,200 films during their tenure in Santa Barbara, of which approximately 100 survive.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

During this period, the [Loughead Aircraft Company](/wiki/Alco_Hydro-Aeroplane_Company "Alco Hydro-Aeroplane Company") was established on lower State Street, and regularly tested [seaplanes](/wiki/Seaplane "Seaplane") off of East Beach. This was the genesis of what would later become [Lockheed](/wiki/Lockheed_Corporation "Lockheed Corporation").

The magnitude 6.3[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) [earthquake of June 29, 1925](/wiki/1925_Santa_Barbara_earthquake "1925 Santa Barbara earthquake"), the first destructive earthquake in California since the [1906 San Francisco quake](/wiki/1906_San_Francisco_earthquake "1906 San Francisco earthquake"), destroyed much of downtown Santa Barbara and killed 13 people. The earthquake caused infrastructure to collapse including the Sheffield Dam.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The low death toll is attributed to the early hour (6:44 a.m., before most people were out on the streets, vulnerable to falling masonry). While this quake, like the one in 1812, was centered in the Santa Barbara Channel, it caused no tsunami. It came at an opportune time for rebuilding, since a movement for architectural reform and unification around a [Spanish Colonial style](/wiki/Spanish_Colonial_Revival_architecture "Spanish Colonial Revival architecture") was already underway. Under the leadership of [Pearl Chase](/wiki/Pearl_Chase "Pearl Chase"), many of the city's famous buildings rose as part of the rebuilding process, including the [Santa Barbara County Courthouse](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse "Santa Barbara County Courthouse"), sometimes praised as the "most beautiful public building in the United States". In 1907 in northern Santa Barbara county a horrific train accident claimed the lives of 37, the exact cause of which is still unknown. It is still the deadliest disaster in the Santa Barbara history.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

During World War II, Santa Barbara was home to [Marine Corps Air Station Santa Barbara](/wiki/Marine_Corps_Air_Station_Santa_Barbara "Marine Corps Air Station Santa Barbara"), and [Naval Reserve Center Santa Barbara](/wiki/Naval_Reserve_Center_Santa_Barbara "Naval Reserve Center Santa Barbara") at the harbor. Up the coast, west of the city, was the Army's Camp Cooke (the present-day [Vandenberg Space Force Base](/wiki/Vandenberg_Space_Force_Base "Vandenberg Space Force Base")). In the city, Hoff General Hospital treated servicemen wounded in the [Pacific Theatre](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean_theater_of_World_War_II "Pacific Ocean theater of World War II").[[42]](#cite_note-42) On February 23, 1942, not long after the outbreak of war in the Pacific, the Japanese submarine *[I-17](/wiki/Japanese_submarine_I-17 "Japanese submarine I-17")* surfaced offshore and [lobbed 16 shells](/wiki/Bombardment_of_Ellwood "Bombardment of Ellwood") at the [Ellwood Oil Field](/wiki/Ellwood_Oil_Field "Ellwood Oil Field"), about 10 miles (15 km) west of Santa Barbara, in the first shelling attack by an enemy power on the continental U.S. since the [bombardment of Orleans](/wiki/Attack_on_Orleans "Attack on Orleans") in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). Although the shelling was inaccurate and only caused about $500 damage to a catwalk, panic was immediate. Many Santa Barbara residents fled, and land values plummeted to historic lows.

### After World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: After World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Santa_Barbara_Harbor_by_Don_Ramey_Logan.jpg/250px-Santa_Barbara_Harbor_by_Don_Ramey_Logan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Barbara_Harbor_by_Don_Ramey_Logan.jpg)

Aerial view of Santa Barbara Harbor and [Stearns Wharf](/wiki/Stearns_Wharf "Stearns Wharf")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/State_Street%2C_Santa_Barbara_-_panoramio_-_Thomas_Camargo.jpg/250px-State_Street%2C_Santa_Barbara_-_panoramio_-_Thomas_Camargo.jpg)](/wiki/File:State_Street,_Santa_Barbara_-_panoramio_-_Thomas_Camargo.jpg)

State Street, Santa Barbara

After the war many of the servicemen who had seen Santa Barbara returned to stay. The population surged by 10,000 people between the end of the war and 1950. This burst of growth had dramatic consequences for the local economy and infrastructure. [Highway 101](/wiki/U.S._Highway_101 "U.S. Highway 101") was built through town during this period, and newly built [Lake Cachuma](/wiki/Lake_Cachuma "Lake Cachuma") began supplying water via a tunnel dug through the mountains between 1950 and 1956.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Local relations with the oil industry gradually soured through the period. Production at Summerland had ended, Elwood was winding down, and to find new fields oil companies carried out seismic exploration of the Channel using explosives, a controversial practice that local fishermen claimed harmed their catch. The culminating disaster, and one of the formative events in the modern environmental movement, was the [blowout at Union Oil's Platform A](/wiki/1969_Santa_Barbara_oil_spill "1969 Santa Barbara oil spill") on the [Dos Cuadras Field](/wiki/Dos_Cuadras_Offshore_Oil_Field "Dos Cuadras Offshore Oil Field"), about eight miles (13 km) southeast of Santa Barbara in the Santa Barbara Channel, on January 28, 1969. Approximately 100,000 barrels (16,000 m3) of oil surged out of a huge undersea break, fouling hundreds of square miles of ocean and all the coastline from Ventura to Goleta, as well north facing beaches on the Channel Islands. Two legislative consequences of the spill in the next year were the passages of the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) and the [National Environmental Policy Act](/wiki/National_Environmental_Policy_Act "National Environmental Policy Act") (NEPA); locally, outraged citizens formed GOO (Get Oil Out).[[44]](#cite_note-Baker,_p._88-89-44)

Santa Barbara's business community strove to attract development until the surge in the anti-growth movement in the 1970s. Many "clean" industries, especially aerospace firms such as Raytheon and Delco Electronics, moved to town in the 1950s and 1960s, bringing employees from other parts of the U.S. UCSB itself became a major employer.[[44]](#cite_note-Baker,_p._88-89-44) In 1975, the city passed an ordinance restricting growth to a maximum of 85,000 residents, through zoning. Growth in the adjacent Goleta Valley could be shut down by denying water meters to developers seeking permits. As a result of these changes, growth slowed down, but prices rose sharply.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

When voters approved connection to State water supplies in 1991, parts of the city, especially outlying areas, resumed growth, but more slowly than during the boom period of the 1950s and 1960s. While the slower growth preserved the quality of life for most residents and prevented the [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl") notorious in the [Los Angeles basin](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Basin "Los Angeles Basin"), housing in the Santa Barbara area was in short supply, and prices soared: in 2006, only six percent of residents could afford a median-value house. As a result, many people who work in Santa Barbara commute from adjacent, more affordable areas, such as Santa Maria, [Lompoc](/wiki/Lompoc,_California "Lompoc, California"), and Ventura. The resultant traffic on incoming arteries, in particular the stretch of Highway 101 between Ventura and Santa Barbara, is another problem being addressed by long-range planners.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

#### Notable wildfires

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Notable wildfires")]

Since the middle of the twentieth century, several destructive fires have affected Santa Barbara: the 1964 Coyote Fire, which burned 67,000 acres (270 km2) of backcountry along with 106 homes; the smaller, but quickly moving, Sycamore Fire in 1977, which burned 200 homes; the disastrous 1990 [Painted Cave Fire](/wiki/Painted_Cave_Fire "Painted Cave Fire"), which incinerated over 500 homes in only several hours, during an intense [Sundowner wind](/wiki/Sundowner_(wind) "Sundowner (wind)") event; the November 2008 [Tea Fire](/wiki/Montecito_Tea_Fire "Montecito Tea Fire"), which destroyed 210 homes in the foothills of Santa Barbara and [Montecito](/wiki/Montecito,_California "Montecito, California"); and the 2009 [Jesusita Fire](/wiki/Jesusita_Fire "Jesusita Fire") that burned 8,733 acres (35.34 km2) and destroyed 160 homes above the [San Roque](#Neighborhoods) region of Santa Barbara.[[48]](#cite_note-city1-48)[[49]](#cite_note-county1-49)

The [Thomas Fire](/wiki/Thomas_Fire "Thomas Fire") burned from its origins in [Santa Paula](/wiki/Santa_Paula,_California "Santa Paula, California") 60 miles (100 km) to the east of Santa Barbara and consumed 281,893 acres (1,140.78 km2) in Santa Barbara and Ventura counties, most of which consisted of rural land and wilderness areas. The fire started on December 4, 2017, and was 100% contained by January 12, 2018. 1,050 structures were lost in the Thomas Fire, mostly east of Santa Barbara in Ventura County. The Thomas Fire has been the largest Santa Barbara County fire (in damage, not size) ever recorded to date.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Santabarbarastreetscene.jpg/250px-Santabarbarastreetscene.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santabarbarastreetscene.jpg)

Looking north from a Santa Barbara street toward "the Riviera" and the Santa Ynez Mountains beyond

Santa Barbara is located about 90 miles (145 km) west-northwest of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles,_California "Los Angeles, California"), along the [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") coast. This stretch of coast along southern [Santa Barbara County](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_County,_California "Santa Barbara County, California") is sometimes referred to as "The American Riviera",[[50]](#cite_note-50) presumably because its geography and climate are similar to those of areas along the northern [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") coast (especially in southern France) known as the [Riviera](/wiki/Riviera "Riviera"). The [Santa Ynez Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Ynez_Mountains "Santa Ynez Mountains"), an east–west trending range, rise dramatically behind the city, with several peaks exceeding 4,000 feet (1,200 m). Covered with [chaparral](/wiki/Chaparral "Chaparral"), oaks and [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone") [outcrops](/wiki/Outcrop "Outcrop"), they make a scenic backdrop to the town. Sometimes, perhaps once every three years, snow falls on the mountains, but it rarely stays for more than a few days. Nearer to town, directly east and adjacent to [Mission Santa Barbara](/wiki/Mission_Santa_Barbara "Mission Santa Barbara"), is an east–west ridge known locally as "the Riviera", traversed by a road called "Alameda Padre Serra" (shortened APS, which translates to "Father Serra's pathway").

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 42.0 square miles (108.8 km2), of which 19.5 square miles (51 km2) of it is land and 22.5 square miles (58 km2) of it, or 53.56%, is water. The high official figures for water are due to the extension of the city limit into the ocean, including a strip of city reaching out into the sea and inland again to keep the Santa Barbara Airport (SBA) within the city boundary.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

Santa Barbara experiences a [warm-summer Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Warm-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Warm-summer Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csb*, [Trewartha](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification"): *Csbl*) characteristic of coastal California. Santa Barbara's weather was ranked number 1 in the United States in 2023–2024 by *U.S. News & World Report*.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Because the city lies along the ocean and parallel to the predominant westerly winds, sideshore and light onshore breezes moderate temperatures resulting in warmer winters and cooler summers compared with places farther inland.

In the winter, storms reach California, some of which bring heavy rainfall but the [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow") effect of the coastal mountains can at times moderate or enhance the rainfall depending on local storm wind flows. Local rainfall totals can be enhanced by [orographic lift](/wiki/Orographic_lift "Orographic lift") when storms are accompanied by southerly flow pushing moist air over the Santa Ynez mountains, producing greater rainfall than in other coastal areas. [Diurnal temperature variation](/wiki/Diurnal_temperature_variation "Diurnal temperature variation") reaches a maximum in winter due to lower humidity and the absence of summer fog. On average, only 1.7 nights have freezing lows.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In general, summers are warm with very few hot days exceeding 90 °F (32 °C), and winters are comfortable and sometimes warm with occasional cooler days topping out around 54–58 °F (12–14 °C). Most days from December to February have highs of 63–69 °F (17–21 °C), and most days from June to August have highs of 72–84 °F (22–29 °C).

Summers in Santa Barbara are mostly rainless due to the presence of a high-pressure area over the eastern Pacific, but summer showers can happen due to tropical hurricane/Monsoonal flows that rarely reach the region; thunderstorms can also occur during the [North American Monsoon](/wiki/North_American_Monsoon "North American Monsoon"). In the fall, afternoon or evening downslope winds, locally called "[Sundowners](/wiki/Sundowner_winds "Sundowner winds")", can raise temperatures into the high 90s °F (high 30s °C) and drop humidities into the single digits, increasing the chance of wildfires, along with their severity, due to downed power lines, etc., in the foothills north of the city.

Annual rainfall totals are highly variable and in exceptional years (like 1940–1941 and 1997–1998) over 40 inches (1,000 mm) of rain have fallen in a year,[[53]](#cite_note-53) but in dry seasons less than 6 inches (150 mm) is not unheard of. Snow sometimes covers higher elevations of the Santa Ynez Mountains but is extremely rare in the city itself. The most recent accumulating snow to fall near sea level was in January 1949, when approximately 2 inches (5.1 cm) fell in the city.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

## Geology and soils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Geology and soils")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Cliffs_at_Arroyo_Burro_Beach_Park.jpg/250px-Cliffs_at_Arroyo_Burro_Beach_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cliffs_at_Arroyo_Burro_Beach_Park.jpg)

Eastward view of the coastal bluffs of the [Douglas Family Preserve](/wiki/Douglas_Family_Preserve "Douglas Family Preserve") park from [Arroyo Burro Beach](/wiki/Arroyo_Burro_Beach "Arroyo Burro Beach")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/SantaBarbaraCA_ArlingtonPeakFromSkofield_20170911.jpg/250px-SantaBarbaraCA_ArlingtonPeakFromSkofield_20170911.jpg)](/wiki/File:SantaBarbaraCA_ArlingtonPeakFromSkofield_20170911.jpg)

Southern view of [Arlington Peak](/wiki/Arlington_Peak_(California) "Arlington Peak (California)") (elevation 3,250 feet; 990 m)[[58]](#cite_note-pb-58) of the [Santa Ynez Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Ynez_Mountains "Santa Ynez Mountains") from [Skofield Park](/wiki/Skofield_Park "Skofield Park")

The city of Santa Barbara is situated on a coastal plain between the [Santa Ynez Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Ynez_Mountains "Santa Ynez Mountains") and the sea. This coastal plain consists of a complex array of [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") and [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") [alluvial](/wiki/Alluvial "Alluvial") and [colluvial](/wiki/Colluvial "Colluvial") deposits, [marine terraces](/wiki/Marine_terrace "Marine terrace"), debris flows, and [estuarine](/wiki/Estuarine "Estuarine") deposits.[[59]](#cite_note-Norris-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) Soils are mostly well drained brown fine [sandy loam](/wiki/Sandy_loam "Sandy loam") of the Milpitas series.[[61]](#cite_note-61) Rapid geologic uplift is characteristic of the entire region, as evidenced by the coastal bluffs and narrow beaches that are present along most of the coastline.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Downtown Santa Barbara occupies a floodplain between two major geologic faults, the Mission Ridge Fault Zone to the north and the Mesa Fault to the south. The Mission Ridge Fault Zone runs along the range of hills known locally as the "Riviera", and the Mesa Fault defines the northern boundary of the band of hills called the "Mesa". These two faults converge near the Five Points Shopping Center at Los Positas and State Streets. Neither is well exposed, with their locations being inferred from topography, springs, seeps, and well logs.[[63]](#cite_note-63) The Mesa Fault continues southeast offshore into the Santa Barbara Channel; the portion of the fault offshore is believed to have been responsible for the destructive [earthquake of 1925](/wiki/1925_Santa_Barbara_earthquake "1925 Santa Barbara earthquake").[[64]](#cite_note-64) The Mission Ridge Fault trends east–west, being named the More Ranch Fault west of Santa Barbara, and forms the northern boundary of the uplands which include [Isla Vista](/wiki/Isla_Vista,_California "Isla Vista, California"), More Mesa, and the [Hope Ranch](/wiki/Hope_Ranch,_California "Hope Ranch, California") Hills.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Three major sedimentary bedrock units underlie the coastal plain: the [Monterey Formation](/wiki/Monterey_Formation "Monterey Formation"), the [Sisquoc Formation](/wiki/Sisquoc_Formation "Sisquoc Formation"), and the Santa Barbara Formation. The Santa Barbara Formation is one of the main units in the [aquifer](/wiki/Aquifer "Aquifer") underlying the city. Its coarse-grained freshwater-bearing portion, much of which is below sea level, is protected from seawater intrusion by the More Ranch Fault, which has shielded it by uplifting less-permeable rocks between it and the sea. The majority of water wells in the Santa Barbara-Goleta area pull from this geologic unit.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The Santa Ynez Mountains to the north of the city consist of multiple layers of sandstone and [conglomerate](/wiki/Conglomerate_(geology) "Conglomerate (geology)") units dating from the [Jurassic Age](/wiki/Jurassic "Jurassic") to the present, uplifted rapidly since the [Pliocene](/wiki/Pliocene "Pliocene"), upended, and in some areas completely overturned. Rapid uplift has given these mountains their craggy, scenic character, and numerous landslides and debris flows, which form some of the urban and suburban lowland areas, are testament to their geologically active nature.[[67]](#cite_note-Minor-67)

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Architecture")]

See also: [City of Santa Barbara Historic Landmarks](/wiki/City_of_Santa_Barbara_Historic_Landmarks "City of Santa Barbara Historic Landmarks") and [National Register of Historic Places listings in Santa Barbara County, California](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Santa_Barbara_County,_California "National Register of Historic Places listings in Santa Barbara County, California")

The first Monterey-style adobe in California was built on State Street of Santa Barbara by the wealthy merchant Alpheus Thompson.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The dominant architectural themes of Santa Barbara are the [Mediterranean Revival](/wiki/Mediterranean_Revival_architecture "Mediterranean Revival architecture"), [Spanish Colonial Revival](/wiki/Spanish_Colonial_Revival_Style_architecture "Spanish Colonial Revival Style architecture") and the related [Mission Revival style](/wiki/Mission_Revival_style_architecture "Mission Revival style architecture"), encouraged through design guidelines adopted by city leaders after the 1925 earthquake destroyed much of the downtown commercial district.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Residential architectural styles in Santa Barbara reflect the era of their construction. Many late-1800s [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") homes remain downtown and in the "Upper East" neighborhood. [California bungalows](/wiki/California_Bungalow "California Bungalow") are common, built in the early decades of the 20th century. Spanish Colonial Revival-style homes built after 1925 are common all over the city, especially in newer upscale residential areas like [Montecito](/wiki/Montecito,_California "Montecito, California") and [Hope Ranch](/wiki/Hope_Ranch,_California "Hope Ranch, California"). Notable modernist and contemporary homes can be found as well.

## Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

View of The Waterfront from W Cabrillo Blvd in Santa Barbara

[![View of the Eastside and Riviera in Santa Barbara, California from the Stearns Wharf](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/View_of_Santa_Barbara%2C_California_from_the_Stearns_Wharf.jpg/250px-View_of_Santa_Barbara%2C_California_from_the_Stearns_Wharf.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_Santa_Barbara,_California_from_the_Stearns_Wharf.jpg)

Santa Barbara has a range of neighborhoods with distinctive histories, architecture, and culture. The significant consensus among locals as to the [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular_geography "Vernacular geography") names and boundaries of these neighborhoods is generally well-reflected by local media outlets' regular use of them in daily reporting. These widely accepted neighborhood definitions differ somewhat from the City of Santa Barbara's formal administrative maps of city neighborhoods which are sometimes more granular and little used in common parlance or media reporting.[[70]](#cite_note-70) Specific municipal service providers such as police, fire, public utilities, and private real estate entities may also use slightly varying neighborhood definitions.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The following is a list of the most widely accepted local neighborhoods, grouped by region.

### Downtown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Downtown")]

* **Downtown** is the central commercial district of Santa Barbara and hosts the highest density of restaurants, bars, and nightlife. It is generally bounded by the Granada Theatre in the Northwest, down State Street to the beach.
* **Lower State Street** is the portion of downtown from the area of [Plaza de la Guerra](/wiki/Plaza_de_la_Guerra "Plaza de la Guerra") (home to city hall) down to the beach. It hosts the highest density of restaurants, bars, and clubs and includes the [Santa Barbara train station](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_station "Santa Barbara station") and Funk Zone district, known for breweries and tasting rooms.
* **The Waterfront** comprises commercial and tourist-oriented business structures along Cabrillo Boulevard including [Stearns Wharf](/wiki/Stearns_Wharf "Stearns Wharf"), the Santa Barbara Harbor, and the breakwater, and extending east toward the Bird Refuge and west along Shoreline Drive above the Santa Barbara City College campus West.

### The Eastside

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: The Eastside")]

* **The Lower Eastside** begins east of State Street and runs northeast to the base of the Riviera. It generally includes [Santa Barbara High School](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_High_School "Santa Barbara High School"), [Santa Barbara Junior High School](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Junior_High_School "Santa Barbara Junior High School"), and the [Santa Barbara Bowl](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Bowl "Santa Barbara Bowl"). The Milpas Street corridor, running from the Santa Barbara Bowl to the waterfront, is the second most important commercial strip in town, after State Street.[[72]](#cite_note-72)
* **The Upper Eastside** begins east of State Street around [Alameda Park](/wiki/Alameda_Park "Alameda Park") and [Alice Keck Park](/wiki/Alice_Keck_Park_Memorial_Garden "Alice Keck Park Memorial Garden") and comprises a primarily residential area bounded in the Northeast by [Mission Santa Barbara](/wiki/Mission_Santa_Barbara "Mission Santa Barbara") and Alameda Padre Serra (APS) road at the base of the Riviera.
* **The Riviera** encompasses an ocean-facing hillside and back hillside extending for approximately two miles. The north side extends from Foothill Road to Sycamore Canyon Road, and the south side extends from the Santa Barbara Mission to Salinas Street. The ribbon-like Alameda Padre Serra road serves as the principal artery for the neighborhood. The area has been known as the "American Riviera" since at least the latter half of the 19th century due to its resemblance to the Mediterranean coastal towns of Italy and France.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The neighborhood has winding streets with intricate stonework terracing built by early 20th-century Italian immigrants. Most of the topography of the Riviera is relatively steep, making it particularly noteworthy for homes with outstanding views of the City of Santa Barbara and the Pacific Ocean.
* [**Mission Canyon**](/wiki/Mission_Canyon,_California "Mission Canyon, California") contains the wooded hilly area beginning at Mission Santa Barbara and extending along Foothill Road, north and east into Mission Canyon Road and Las Canoas Road. A popular spot as an entry-point for weekend foothill hiking, it is a rustically beautiful, though fire-prone area of Santa Barbara due to heavy natural vegetation. The area is administratively outside of Santa Barbara City limits.

### The Westside

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: The Westside")]

* **The Westside** begins west of State Street and is bounded on its western and southwestern flanks by the Mesa. The area includes residential and commercial stretches on both sides of Highway 101, and reaches down to Cliff Drive, incorporating [Santa Barbara City College](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_City_College "Santa Barbara City College"). At its northern extent, it includes the 'Oak Park' neighborhood and abuts Upper State Street.
* **The Mesa** stretches 2.5 miles (4.0 km) from Santa Barbara City College in the east to Arroyo Burro County Beach (or "Hendry's/The Pit" to locals) on the west. The neighborhood has beach access to Mesa Lane Beach, as well as Thousand Steps Beach. Residential development began here in the 1920s but was interrupted by the discovery of the [Mesa Oil Field](/wiki/Mesa_Oil_Field "Mesa Oil Field"). The field was quickly exhausted, and after the Second World War, building of houses resumed, although the last oil tanks and sumps did not disappear until the early 1970s.[[74]](#cite_note-Easton-74)
* **Alta Mesa and Bel Air** comprise most of the coastal highlands of Santa Barbara, north (landward) of the Mesa. The area is almost entirely residential and includes Honda Valley Park and [Elings Park](/wiki/Elings_Park "Elings Park"). The area's northern slope provides notable views of downtown Santa Barbara, the Riviera, the [Santa Ynez Mountains](/wiki/Santa_Ynez_Mountains "Santa Ynez Mountains"), and the coast towards Ventura. The southern slope provides views of the [Santa Barbara Channel](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Channel "Santa Barbara Channel") and [Channel Islands National Park](/wiki/Channel_Islands_National_Park "Channel Islands National Park"). Due to its position along Santa Barbara County's east–west-trending southern coastline, fall and winter sunrises occur over the ocean, a rarity on the Pacific coast of the United States.
* **Samarkand** is a residential area home to about 2,000 inhabitants. The name Samarkand is derived from old Persian, meaning "the land of heart's desire" and was first applied to a deluxe Persian-style hotel converted from a boys' school in the area in 1920.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Samarkand later became the moniker for the general neighborhood located between Las Positas Road, State Street, De La Vina Street, Oak Park, and Highway 101.

### Upper State Street

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Upper State Street")]

* **Upper State Street** is the portion of State Street running east to west from the San Roque area in the east to just past the interchange of Highway 101 and [California State Route 154](/wiki/California_State_Route_154 "California State Route 154") in the west. It is a primarily commercial corridor bounded by San Roque to its North and Highway 101 to its south. It also generally includes the "Hitchcock" area, comprising a residential portion, as well as the Earl Warren Showgrounds, and an adjacent golf course.
* **[San Roque](/wiki/San_Roque,_Santa_Barbara,_California "San Roque, Santa Barbara, California")** is located northwest of the downtown area, bounded to its south by Upper State Street and to its north by the foothills. The area is considered to be somewhat more temperate than surrounding neighborhoods due to its relative distance from the ocean and shielding by the low coastal hills to the south of the 101 freeway. The area is almost exclusively residential except where it abuts the commercial corridor of Upper State Street.

The [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") counted 88,665 people, 35,383 households, and 19,257 families in Santa Barbara.[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-:0-82) The population density was 4,545.3 people per square mile (1,755.0 people/km2). There were 38,208 housing units at an average density of 1,958.7 units per square mile (756.3 units/km2).[[82]](#cite_note-:0-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83) The racial makeup was 58.47% (51,842) [white](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)") or [European American](/wiki/European_American "European American") (51.75% [non-Hispanic white](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_White "Non-Hispanic White")), 1.4% (1,238) [black](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)") or [African-American](/wiki/African_American "African American"), 1.49% (1,320) [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)") or [Alaska Native](/wiki/Alaska_Native "Alaska Native"), 3.59% (3,184) [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.09% (80) [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)") or [Native Hawaiian](/wiki/Native_Hawaiian "Native Hawaiian"), 17.62% (15,623) from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 17.34% (15,378) from [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans").[[84]](#cite_note-84) [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race was 38.73% (34,344) of the population.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Of the 35,383 households, 23.5% had children under the age of 18; 38.5% were married couples living together; 31.2% had a female householder with no spouse or partner present. 32.3% of households consisted of individuals and 14.3% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.[[82]](#cite_note-:0-82) The average household size was 2.4 and the average family size was 3.0.[[86]](#cite_note-86) The percent of those with a bachelor's degree or higher was estimated to be 38.9% of the population.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

16.6% of the population was under the age of 18, 9.8% from 18 to 24, 29.4% from 25 to 44, 24.1% from 45 to 64, and 20.1% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 40.2 years. For every 100 females, there were 104.5 males.[[82]](#cite_note-:0-82) For every 100 females ages 18 and older, there were 105.7 males.[[82]](#cite_note-:0-82)

The 2016–2020 5-year [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") estimates show that the median household income was $81,618 (with a margin of error of +/- $3,653) and the median family income was $105,513 (+/- $9,757).[[88]](#cite_note-88) Males had a median income of $44,225 (+/- $2,697) versus $33,978 (+/- $2,747) for females. The median income for those above 16 years old was $40,200 (+/- $1,911).[[89]](#cite_note-89) Approximately, 7.2% of families and 12.3% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 11.2% of those under the age of 18 and 8.5% of those ages 65 or over.[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)

Santa Barbara is home to various religious institutions, including [Mission Santa Barbara](/wiki/Mission_Santa_Barbara "Mission Santa Barbara") and [Congregation B'nai B'rith](/wiki/Congregation_B%27nai_B%27rith_(Santa_Barbara,_California) "Congregation B'nai B'rith (Santa Barbara, California)"), a Reform Jewish synagogue founded in 1927.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

According to the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census")[[93]](#cite_note-auto-93) the [racial makeup](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)") of Santa Barbara was 66,411 (75.1%) White; 1,420 (1.6%) African American; 892 (1.0%) Native American; 3,062 (3.5%) Asian (1.0% Chinese, 0.6% Filipino, 0.5% Japanese, 0.4% Korean, 0.4% Indian, 0.2% Vietnamese, 0.4% other); 116 (0.1%) Pacific Islander; 13,032 (14.7%) from other races; 3,477 (3.9%) from two or more races. Hispanics or Latinos of any race were 33,591 persons (38.0%). [Non-Hispanic](/wiki/European-Americans "European-Americans") Whites were 45,852 persons (52.2%)

The Census reported that 86,783 people (98.2% of the population) lived in households, 1,172 (1.3%) lived in non-institutionalized group quarters, and 455 (0.5%) were institutionalized.

Of the 35,449 households, 8,768 (24.7%) had children under the age of 18 living in them, 13,240 (37.3%) were [opposite-sex married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 3,454 (9.7%) had a female householder with no husband present, and 1,539 (4.3%) had a male householder with no wife present. There were 2,420 (6.8%) [unmarried opposite-sex partnerships](/wiki/POSSLQ "POSSLQ"), and 339 (1.0%) [same-sex married couples or partnerships](/wiki/Same-sex_partnerships "Same-sex partnerships"); 11,937 households (33.7%) were made up of individuals, and 4,340 (12.2%) had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.45. There were 18,233 [families](/wiki/Family_(U.S._Census) "Family (U.S. Census)") (51.4% of all households); the average family size was 3.13.

The age distribution of the city was the following: 16,468 people (18.6%) under the age of 18, 10,823 people (12.2%) aged 18 to 24, 26,241 people (29.7%) aged 25 to 44, 22,305 people (25.2%) aged 45 to 64, and 12,573 people (14.2%) who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 36.8 years. For every 100 females, there were 98.5 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 97.7 males.

There were 37,820 housing units at an average density of 901.2 units per square mile (348.0 units/km2), of which 13,784 (38.9%) were owner-occupied, and 21,665 (61.1%) were occupied by renters. The homeowner vacancy rate was 1.3%; the rental vacancy rate was 4.1%; 34,056 people (38.5% of the population) lived in owner-occupied housing units and 52,727 people (59.6%) lived in rental housing units.

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [census](/wiki/Census "Census")[[94]](#cite_note-GR2-94) of 2000, 92,325 people\*, 35,605 households, and 18,941 families resided in the city. The population density was 4,865.3 inhabitants per square mile (1,878.5/km2). There were 37,076 housing units at an average density of 1,953.8 units per square mile (754.4 units/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 74.0% White, 1.8% African American, 1.1% Native American, 2.8% Asian, 0.1% Pacific Islander, 16.4% from other races, and 3.9% from two or more races. People of Hispanic or Latino background, of any race, were 35.0% of the population.

Of the 35,605 households, 24.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 39.8% were married couples living together, 9.5% had a female householder with no husband present, and 46.8% were not families. About 32.9% of all households were made up of individuals, and 11.4% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.47 and the average family size was 3.17.

In the city, the population was distributed as 19.8% under the age of 18, 13.8% from 18 to 24, 32.3% from 25 to 44, 20.4% from 45 to 64, and 13.8% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 35 years. For every 100 females, there were 97.0 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 95.0 males.

The median [income](/wiki/Income "Income") for a household in the city was $47,498, and for a family was $57,880. Males had a median income of $37,116 versus $31,911 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $26,466. About 7.7% of families and 13.4% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 16.8% of those under age 18 and 7.4% of those age 65 or over. If one compares the [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") to the actual cost of living, the number of people living below the poverty line is considerably higher.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Economy")]

Aerospace and defense companies such as [Alliant Techsystems](/wiki/Alliant_Techsystems "Alliant Techsystems"), [Channel Technologies Group](/wiki/Channel_Technologies_Group "Channel Technologies Group"), [FLIR Systems](/wiki/FLIR_Systems "FLIR Systems"), and [Raytheon](/wiki/Raytheon "Raytheon") have major operations in the area. As a tourist destination, the hospitality industry has a significant presence in the regional economy. Among notable business ventures and innovations, [Motel 6](/wiki/Motel_6 "Motel 6") was started in Santa Barbara in 1962.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) [Sambo's](/wiki/Sambo%27s "Sambo's") Restaurant was founded in Santa Barbara in 1957 by Sam Battistone and Newell Bohnett. The [Egg McMuffin](/wiki/McMuffin "McMuffin") was invented by [Herb Peterson](/wiki/Herb_Peterson "Herb Peterson") at the upper State Street [McDonald's](/wiki/McDonald%27s "McDonald's").[[97]](#cite_note-97) [The Habit Burger Grill](/wiki/The_Habit_Burger_Grill "The Habit Burger Grill") restaurant chain began in Old Town [Goleta](/wiki/Goleta,_California "Goleta, California").[[98]](#cite_note-98) [Kinko's](/wiki/Kinko%27s "Kinko's") (now owned by [FedEx](/wiki/FedEx "FedEx") and known as [FedEx Office](/wiki/FedEx_Office "FedEx Office")) was founded by Paul Orfalea in Isla Vista, near [UC Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara"), in 1970.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

### Performing arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Performing arts")]

Santa Barbara contains numerous performing art venues, including the 2,000-seat [Arlington Theatre](/wiki/Arlington_Theatre "Arlington Theatre"), which is the largest indoor performance venue in Santa Barbara and the site of the annual [Santa Barbara International Film Festival](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_International_Film_Festival "Santa Barbara International Film Festival").[[104]](#cite_note-104) Other major venues include the [Lobero Theatre](/wiki/Lobero_Theatre "Lobero Theatre"), a historic building and favorite venue for small concerts; the Granada Theater, the tallest building downtown, originally built by contractor C.B. Urton in 1924, but with the theatre remodeled and reopened in March 2008; and the [Santa Barbara Bowl](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Bowl "Santa Barbara Bowl"), a 4,562-seat outdoor amphitheater in a canyon at the base of the Riviera.

The city is considered a haven for [classical music](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music") lovers with the [Santa Barbara Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Symphony_Orchestra "Santa Barbara Symphony Orchestra"), a professional opera company,[[105]](#cite_note-105) and many non-profit classical music groups (such as [CAMA](/wiki/Community_Arts_Music_Association "Community Arts Music Association")). Several youth orchestras are also located in Santa Barbara, such as the Santa Barbara Youth Symphony.[[106]](#cite_note-106) The [Music Academy of the West](/wiki/Music_Academy_of_the_West "Music Academy of the West"), located in [Montecito](/wiki/Montecito,_California "Montecito, California"), hosts an annual music festival in the summer, drawing renowned students and professionals.

Santa Barbara is a year-round tourist destination renowned for its fair weather, downtown beaches, and Spanish architecture. Tourism brings more than one billion dollars per year into the local economy, including $80 million in tax revenue.[[107]](#cite_note-107) The waterfront along Cabrillo Boulevard is a draw for tourists centered on [Stearns Wharf](/wiki/Stearns_Wharf "Stearns Wharf"). The pier features shops, restaurants, and the [Ty Warner Sea Center](/wiki/Ty_Warner_Sea_Center "Ty Warner Sea Center").[[108]](#cite_note-108)[[109]](#cite_note-109)

[Mission Santa Barbara](/wiki/Mission_Santa_Barbara "Mission Santa Barbara"), "The Queen of the Missions", is an active [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan") mission and place of worship, sightseeing stop, and [national historic landmark](/wiki/National_historic_landmark "National historic landmark"). Annually over the Memorial Day weekend, there is a chalk-art festival known as *I Madonnari*, with ephemeral works of art created on the asphalt in front of the mission, and food stalls set up and music.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

The [Santa Barbara County Courthouse](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_County_Courthouse "Santa Barbara County Courthouse"), a red tiled Spanish-Moorish structure, provides a view of the downtown area from its open-air tower. Its surrounding gardens, known as the Sunken Gardens, are a popular location for many events, from weddings to city-hosted summer movie nights in the park.[[111]](#cite_note-111) The [Presidio of Santa Barbara](/wiki/Presidio_of_Santa_Barbara "Presidio of Santa Barbara"), a Spanish military installation and chapel built in 1782, was central to the town's early development and colonial roots. In 1855, the Presidio Chapel, being in decay, grew into the Apostolic College of Our Lady of Sorrows, now [Our Lady of Sorrows Church](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Sorrows_Church_(Santa_Barbara,_California) "Our Lady of Sorrows Church (Santa Barbara, California)").[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Events")]

The annual Fiesta (originally called "Old Spanish Days") is celebrated every year in August. The Fiesta is hosted by the [Native Daughters of the Golden West](/wiki/Native_Daughters_of_the_Golden_West "Native Daughters of the Golden West") and the [Native Sons of the Golden West](/wiki/Native_Sons_of_the_Golden_West "Native Sons of the Golden West") in a joint committee called the Fiesta Board. Fiesta was originally started as a tourist attraction, like the [Rose Bowl](/wiki/Rose_Bowl_Game "Rose Bowl Game"), to draw business into the town in the 1920s.[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114) Flower Girls and Las Señoritas march and participate in both Fiesta Pequeña (the kickoff of Fiesta) and the various parades. Flower Girls is for girls under 13. They throw roses and other flowers into the crowds. Las Señoritas are their older escorts. Many Señoritas join the Native Daughters at the age of 16.

The annual Santa Barbara French Festival takes place on [Bastille Day](/wiki/Bastille_Day "Bastille Day") weekend in July and is the largest French Festival in the western United States.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

New Noise Music Conference and Festival, established in 2009, is a four-day event with the main party in the Funk Zone, a small art and wine tasting section of the city near the beach, and other small bands to local venues around the city. New Noise brings in over 75 bands and 50 speakers to the festival each year.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

For over 40 years, the Santa Barbara Arts and Crafts Show has been held on Cabrillo Boulevard, east of [Stearns Wharf](/wiki/Stearns_Wharf "Stearns Wharf") and along the beach, attracting thousands of people to see artwork made by artists and crafts people that live in Santa Barbara County. By the rules of the show, all the works displayed must have been made by the artists and craftspeople themselves, who sell their own goods. The show started in the early 1960s and now has over 200 booths on Sundays. The show is also held on some Saturdays that are national holidays, but not during inclement weather.

The [Santa Barbara International Film Festival](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_International_Film_Festival "Santa Barbara International Film Festival"), another local non-profit, draws over 50,000 attendees during what is usually Santa Barbara's slow season in late January. SBIFF hosts a wide variety of celebrities, premieres, panels and movies from around the world and runs for 10 days.

The annual [Summer Solstice Parade](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Summer_Solstice_Parade "Santa Barbara Summer Solstice Parade") draws up to 100,000 people.[[117]](#cite_note-117) It is a colorful themed parade put on by local residents, and follows a route along Santa Barbara Street (formerly State Street) for approximately one mile, ending at [Alameda Park](/wiki/Alameda_Park "Alameda Park"). Its main rule is that no written messages or banners with words are allowed. Floats and costumes vary from the whimsical to the outrageous; parties and street events take place throughout the weekend of the parade, the first weekend after the solstice.

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Casa_de_la_Guerra_right_side.jpg/250px-Casa_de_la_Guerra_right_side.jpg)](/wiki/File:Casa_de_la_Guerra_right_side.jpg)

[Casa de la Guerra](/wiki/Casa_de_la_Guerra "Casa de la Guerra") is currently open as a museum.

The [Santa Barbara Museum of Art](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Museum_of_Art "Santa Barbara Museum of Art") (SBMA), located on State Street, features nationally recognized collections and special exhibitions of international importance. Highlights of the Museum's permanent collection include antiquities; 19th-century French, British, and American art; 20th-century and contemporary European, North American, and Latin American art; Asian art; photography; and works on paper. It has an education program that serves local and surrounding communities through extensive on-site programming and curriculum resources.

[Museum of Contemporary Art Santa Barbara](http://www.mcasantabarbara.org) (MCASB), located on the top floor of Paseo Nuevo shopping mall, is a non-profit, non-collecting museum dedicated to the exhibition, education, and cultivation of the arts of our time. It offers free admission to its exhibitions and public programming.

Other art venues include the [University Art Museum](/wiki/University_art_museum,_Santa_Barbara "University art museum, Santa Barbara") on the University of California at Santa Barbara Campus, various private galleries, and a wide variety of art and photography shows. The [Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Museum_of_Natural_History "Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History") is located immediately behind the [Santa Barbara Mission](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Mission "Santa Barbara Mission") in a complex of Mission-style buildings set in a park-like campus. The Museum offers indoor and outdoor exhibits and a state-of-the-art planetarium.

The [Santa Barbara Historical Museum](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Historical_Museum "Santa Barbara Historical Museum") is located on De La Guerra Street. The Santa Barbara Maritime Museum is located at 113 Harbor Way (the former [Naval Reserve Center Santa Barbara](/wiki/Naval_Reserve_Center_Santa_Barbara "Naval Reserve Center Santa Barbara")) on the waterfront. The [Karpeles Manuscript Library Museum](/wiki/Karpeles_Manuscript_Library_Museum "Karpeles Manuscript Library Museum") (free admission) houses a collection of historical documents and manuscripts. Two open-air museums here are [Lotusland](/wiki/Lotusland "Lotusland") and [Casa del Herrero](/wiki/Casa_del_Herrero "Casa del Herrero"), exemplifying the American Country Place era in Santa Barbara. [Casa Dolores](/wiki/Casa_Dolores "Casa Dolores"), center for the popular arts of Mexico, is devoted to the collection, preservation, study, and exhibition of an extensive variety of objects of the popular arts of Mexico.

The Reagan Ranch Center is a three-story museum and gallery operated by [Young America's Foundation](/wiki/Young_America%27s_Foundation "Young America's Foundation"), next to the Amtrak Station on Lower State Street. Its focus is the history of the [Rancho del Cielo](/wiki/Rancho_del_Cielo "Rancho del Cielo") and the role it played in Ronald Reagan's life.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

[MOXI, The Wolf Museum of Exploration + Innovation](/wiki/MOXI,_The_Wolf_Museum_of_Exploration_%2B_Innovation "MOXI, The Wolf Museum of Exploration + Innovation") is a [non-profit](/wiki/Non-profit "Non-profit") [science](/wiki/Science_museum "Science museum") and [children's](/wiki/Children%27s_museum "Children's museum") museum in Santa Barbara. A 501(c)(3) non-profit, the museum opened on February 25th, 2017.[[119]](#cite_note-NZHK-119)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Sports")]

Athletics teams wearing the [UC Santa Barbara](/wiki/UC_Santa_Barbara_Gauchos "UC Santa Barbara Gauchos") Gauchos uniform are some of the most popular spectator sports locally. The Gauchos field 20 varsity teams in [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I"), most of which play in the [Big West Conference](/wiki/Big_West_Conference "Big West Conference"). Popular teams include the [men's soccer team](/wiki/UC_Santa_Barbara_Gauchos_men%27s_soccer "UC Santa Barbara Gauchos men's soccer"), which averages over 3,800 fans per game,[[120]](#cite_note-120) and the [men's basketball team](/wiki/UC_Santa_Barbara_Gauchos_men%27s_basketball "UC Santa Barbara Gauchos men's basketball"), which averages over 2,300 fans per game.[[121]](#cite_note-121)

Santa Barbara annually hosts the *[Semana Nautica](/wiki/Semana_Nautica_15K "Semana Nautica 15K")* Summer Sports Festival.[[122]](#cite_note-122) One of the main events of the festival is the [Semana Nautica 15K](/wiki/Semana_Nautica_15K "Semana Nautica 15K"), the oldest continuously running race on California's central coast.[[123]](#cite_note-123) Nite Moves is a popular local 5k race, with an optional ocean swim portion, open to all ages and held on Wednesday evenings from May to the end of August.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

Surfing is a part of Santa Barbara culture. The late [Bruce Brown](/wiki/Bruce_Brown_(director) "Bruce Brown (director)")'s cult classic documentary, *[The Endless Summer](/wiki/The_Endless_Summer "The Endless Summer")*, put surfing on the map, and he was often seen around town prior to his death in December 2017. Surfing legend Pat Curren and his son, three-time world champion [Tom Curren](/wiki/Tom_Curren "Tom Curren"), as well as ten-time world champion [Kelly Slater](/wiki/Kelly_Slater "Kelly Slater"), and other popular surfers such as Shaun Tompson, [Jack Johnson](/wiki/Jack_Johnson_(musician) "Jack Johnson (musician)") and Chris Brown call Santa Barbara home. The Channel Islands block summer surf swells that come from the tropics or further south, the southern hemisphere. For these reasons Santa Barbara is viewed as a winter surf location.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Government")]

In 2015, the city council voted to change from at-large elections to district elections for city council seats.[[126]](#cite_note-126)

All of Santa Barbara County falls into [California's 24th congressional district](/wiki/California%27s_24th_congressional_district "California's 24th congressional district"). The district leans towards the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), with a [PVI](/wiki/Cook_Partisan_Voting_Index "Cook Partisan Voting Index") of D+10,[[127]](#cite_note-127) making it politically aligned with the rest of California overall. The current Representative is [Salud Carbajal](/wiki/Salud_Carbajal "Salud Carbajal").

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Media")]

### Newspapers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Newspapers")]

The Santa Barbara area is served by several news outlets:

* The [Santa Barbara News-Press](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_News-Press "Santa Barbara News-Press"), founded in 1868, is the area's oldest news outlet. It returned to daily digital publication in 2025 following a hiatus stemming from a 2023 bankruptcy. Prior to this, the paper received international attention and became the subject of the 2008 documentary, "Citizen McCaw" due to controversial actions taken by its former owner, [Wendy McCaw](/wiki/Wendy_McCaw "Wendy McCaw").[[150]](#cite_note-150)[[151]](#cite_note-151)
* The [Santa Barbara Independent](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Independent "Santa Barbara Independent"), founded in 1986, publishes a tabloid format print edition weekly on Thursdays and claims an audited print readership of 68,000.[[152]](#cite_note-152) It also publishes as an online daily.
* VOICE Magazine (Voice SB), founded in 1994, publishes a tabloid format print edition weekly on Fridays and claims a total readership of over 51,000.[[153]](#cite_note-153) It also publishes an e-edition online.[[154]](#cite_note-154)

Santa Barbara is also served by two online-only news outlets:

* *Edhat*, a privately owned community news site founded in 2003.[[155]](#cite_note-155)
* *[Noozhawk](/wiki/Noozhawk "Noozhawk")*, an online news site founded in 2007

In addition, the business journal *Pacific Coast Business Times* covers Ventura, Santa Barbara, and San Luis Obispo counties.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Barbara,_California&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Santa_Barbara_train_station%2C_California%2C_7_March_2007.jpg/250px-Santa_Barbara_train_station%2C_California%2C_7_March_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Santa_Barbara_train_station,_California,_7_March_2007.jpg)

[Santa Barbara station](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_station "Santa Barbara station"), built in 1902 by the [Southern Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Southern_Pacific_Railroad "Southern Pacific Railroad") in the [Spanish Mission Revival style](/wiki/Mission_Revival_Style_architecture "Mission Revival Style architecture")

Santa Barbara is bisected by [U.S. Route 101](/wiki/U.S._Route_101 "U.S. Route 101"), an automotive transportation corridor that links the city to the rest of the Central Coast region, [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") to the north, and [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") to the southeast. [Santa Barbara Municipal Airport](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Municipal_Airport "Santa Barbara Municipal Airport") offers commercial air service. Santa Barbara Aviation provides locally based private jet charter aircraft. [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") offers rail service through the *[Coast Starlight](/wiki/Coast_Starlight "Coast Starlight")* and *[Pacific Surfliner](/wiki/Pacific_Surfliner "Pacific Surfliner")* trains at the [Santa Barbara station](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_station "Santa Barbara station") on State Street, and another stop at [Goleta Station](/wiki/Goleta_station "Goleta station").

The [Santa Barbara Metropolitan Transit District](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_Metropolitan_Transit_District "Santa Barbara Metropolitan Transit District") (MTD) provides local bus service across the city. [Greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") bus lines provide service to downtown Santa Barbara. Electric shuttles operated by MTD ferry tourists and shoppers up and down lower State Street and to the wharf. The Clean Air Express bus offers connections to Lompoc and Santa Maria. [Ventura Intercity Service Transit Authority](/wiki/Ventura_Intercity_Service_Transit_Authority "Ventura Intercity Service Transit Authority") (VISTA) bus service offers connections south to Ventura and west to Goleta. Santa Barbara Airbus offers daily service to/from [LAX](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport") from downtown Santa Barbara, [Carpinteria](/wiki/Carpinteria,_California "Carpinteria, California") and [Goleta](/wiki/Goleta,_California "Goleta, California").

Santa Barbara has an extensive network of bike trails and other resources for cyclists,[[157]](#cite_note-157) and the [League of American Bicyclists](/wiki/League_of_American_Bicyclists "League of American Bicyclists") recognizes Santa Barbara as a Silver Level city. Santa Barbara Car Free promotes visiting and exploring the area without the use of a car.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

Often chosen as a winter training location for professional cycling teams and [snowbirds](/wiki/Snowbird_(person) "Snowbird (person)"), Santa Barbara has cycling routes and several climbs, including Gibraltar Road and Old San Marcos/Painted Cave. A bike path and route connect the University of California, Santa Barbara to the downtown area, passing through Goleta and Hope Ranch. Bike rentals are a way for tourists to view Santa Barbara and the surrounding area. In 2009, the Santa Barbara-Santa Maria-Goleta metropolitan statistical area (MSA) ranked as the sixth highest in the United States for the percentage of commuters who biked to work (4 percent).[[159]](#cite_note-159)

From 1875 until 1929, a [streetcar network](/wiki/Streetcars_in_Santa_Barbara,_California "Streetcars in Santa Barbara, California") existed in Santa Barbara.[[160]](#cite_note-160)

* *[20th Century Women](/wiki/20th_Century_Women "20th Century Women")*, a 2016 American comedy-drama
* *[Psych 2: Lassie Come Home](/wiki/Psych_2:_Lassie_Come_Home "Psych 2: Lassie Come Home")*, a 2020 American made-for-television comedy

*[Psych](/wiki/Psych "Psych")* was set in Santa Barbara, though [White Rock](/wiki/White_Rock,_British_Columbia "White Rock, British Columbia"), British Columbia, Canada, was used as the filming location.[[166]](#cite_note-166)

*[Santa Barbara](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_(TV_series) "Santa Barbara (TV series)"),* the popular soap opera set locally, aired on NBC from 1984 to 1993.[[167]](#cite_note-167)

The TV series *[Owen Marshall, Counselor at Law](/wiki/Owen_Marshall,_Counselor_at_Law "Owen Marshall, Counselor at Law")* was set in Santa Barbara.

*[An American Family](/wiki/An_American_Family "An American Family")*, which is known as the first American reality television show, chronicled the daily lives of a family living in Santa Barbara.[[168]](#cite_note-168)

A portion of the 2020 video game *[The Last of Us Part II](/wiki/The_Last_of_Us_II "The Last of Us II")* takes place in Santa Barbara.[[169]](#cite_note-169)

In 2022, shooting began on season 4 of the reality TV series *[Love Island](/wiki/Love_Island_(American_season_4) "Love Island (American season 4)"),* which is set at Dos Pueblos Ranch, a 214-acre ranch west of [Goleta](/wiki/Goleta,_California "Goleta, California").

Santa Barbara appears multiple times in the American animated series [BoJack Horseman](/wiki/BoJack_Horseman "BoJack Horseman").
